senses.definition: Archaic: bound to someone by a promise of marriage or strong faith.
examples: They stood before the altar, already affied in their hearts.
Two souls affied in trust can face any hardship together.

senses.definition: Archaic past tense of a verb meaning to trust someone or to promise yourself to them.
examples: She affied herself to his care and followed him into exile.
He affied his future to the wisdom of his guides.

senses.definition: Non‑standard or rare: third person singular form of a verb meaning to pledge yourself to or trust someone.
examples: He affies his fortunes to that risky venture.
She affies her hopes to the promise of peace.

senses.definition: Very rare: to polish or refine something, especially in style or character.
examples: Time will affile his rough manners.
The editor helped to affile the author's early drafts.

senses.definition: Able to be connected with or attached to an organization or group.
examples: The smaller clinics are affiliable to the central hospital system.
Only affiliable institutions may apply for funding under this scheme.

senses.definition: To officially join or connect a person or organization with a larger group.
examples: Several local clubs decided to affiliate with the national association.
The college is affiliated with a well‑known university.

senses.definition: A company, organization, or person that is closely connected to a larger organization.
examples: The TV station is a local affiliate of a major American network.
Our overseas affiliates handle sales in foreign markets.

senses.definition: Officially connected with or controlled by a larger group or organization.
examples: Only affiliated members can vote in the meeting.
The charity is affiliated with an international relief organization.

senses.definition: Companies, organizations, or people that are officially connected to a larger group.
examples: The corporation has affiliates in more than twenty countries.
Local affiliates will handle distribution and customer support.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of a verb meaning to officially join or connect to a larger group.
examples: The new sports club affiliates with the national federation.
Any organization that affiliates with the council must follow its rules.

senses.definition: Present participle of a verb meaning joining or connecting with a larger group or organization.
examples: Many small companies are affiliating with larger brands to survive.
By affiliating with the league, the club gains more recognition.

senses.definition: A formal connection with a group, organization, or political party.
examples: Membership is open to people of any political affiliation.
Applicants must state their institutional affiliation on the form.

senses.definition: Plural of affiliation: several formal connections with groups or organizations.
examples: He listed his professional affiliations at the end of the article.
Their social and political affiliations strongly influence their views.

senses.definition: The process of caring for and aging cheese to improve its flavor and texture.
examples: He specializes in affinage and works with dozens of traditional cheeses.
Good affinage can turn an average cheese into something exceptional.

senses.definition: Obsolete: the process of refining metal to make it more pure.
examples: In the past, affinage was an important step in preparing gold for coins.
The old workshop once carried out affinage of precious metals.

senses.definition: Relating to relatives by marriage rather than by blood.
examples: Anthropologists distinguish between consanguineal and affinal kin.
She has both blood relatives and affinal relatives living nearby.

senses.definition: The process of purifying or refining a substance, especially sugar or metal.
examples: Affination removes impurities before the sugar is crystallized.
The factory uses modern methods of affination to improve product quality.

senses.definition: In mathematics, relating to transformations that preserve straight lines but not necessarily distances and angles.
examples: An affine transformation can stretch or shear a shape.
Affine geometry studies properties that remain unchanged under such transformations.

senses.definition: In anthropology, a relative by marriage rather than by blood.
examples: In some cultures, affines play a major role in family decision‑making.
The ceremony involved both blood relatives and affines.

senses.definition: Literary: closely connected or bound to someone or something, often by shared nature or feeling.
examples: She felt strangely affined to the ancient landscape.
These two artists seem deeply affined in their vision.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to affine transformations or properties in mathematics.
examples: The points are affinely independent in the given space.
The function behaves affinely over each region.

senses.definition: Relatives by marriage rather than by blood.
examples: In‑laws are considered affines in many kinship systems.
The gathering included both consanguines and affines.

senses.definition: Non‑standard: present participle of a rare verb meaning to pledge yourself or trust.
examples: In the old ballad, she is affing herself to a stranger.
They spoke of affing their lives to a shared cause.

senses.definition: Relating to affinity or a natural connection between things.
examples: The researcher explored the affinitative links between the two languages.
There is an affinitative relationship between these chemical compounds.

senses.definition: In a way that shows or involves a natural connection or affinity.
examples: The two dialects are affinitatively linked.
These ideas are affinitatively arranged in the text.

senses.definition: Rare: something or someone having an affinity or close connection with another.
examples: Each mineral has its own affinite in the series.
She seems an affinite of the earlier poet in style and tone.

senses.definition: Natural liking for or connection with other people, ideas, or things.
examples: He feels strong affinities with minimalist art.
There are clear affinities between these two philosophies.

senses.definition: Obscure: the act of forming an affinity or close connection.
examples: The affinition of the two traditions created a new style.
Some scholars discuss the affinition of languages in this region.

senses.definition: Showing a natural connection or attraction between things.
examples: There is an affinitive bond between the two themes in the novel.
The species have affinitive characteristics in their behavior.

senses.definition: A natural liking for or attraction to a person, thing, or idea.
examples: She has a real affinity for classical music.
He feels an affinity with people who share his values.

senses.definition: A similarity or close relationship between things.
examples: There is a clear affinity between the two languages.
Scientists noted an affinity between the chemical structures.

senses.definition: In chemistry or biology, a strong attraction between substances or molecules.
examples: The drug has a high affinity for these receptors.
Affinity between the molecules makes the reaction possible.

senses.definition: To say firmly that something is true.
examples: She affirmed that she had never seen the document before.
The report affirms the need for further research.

senses.definition: In law, to state that a judgment or decision is correct and will stand.
examples: The higher court affirmed the decision of the lower court.
The ruling was affirmed on appeal.

senses.definition: To support or strengthen someone emotionally by expressing approval or value.
examples: Parents should affirm their children's efforts, not just their successes.
The teacher's kind words affirmed his sense of self‑worth.

senses.definition: That can be stated firmly as true.
examples: The facts in this case are clearly affirmable.
Only affirmable claims should be included in the report.

senses.definition: In a way that can be firmly stated as true.
examples: The hypothesis cannot be affirmably proven with current data.
Only a few of these statements can be affirmably supported.

senses.definition: In law, the act of stating that a judgment or decision is correct and will stand.
examples: The court's affirmance of the verdict ended the long case.
On affirmance, the original judgment became final.

senses.definition: Plural of affirmance: several legal acts of stating a judgment is correct.
examples: The court issued multiple affirmances during that term.
These affirmances show the court's support for the lower rulings.

senses.definition: A person who makes a formal statement that something is true, especially in a legal document.
examples: The affirmant signed the declaration before a notary.
Each affirmant is responsible for the truth of their statement.

senses.definition: People who make formal statements that something is true, especially in legal documents.
examples: The affirmants submitted their statements to the court.
All affirmants must appear in person if requested.

senses.definition: A strong statement that something is true.
examples: Her calm affirmation of the facts helped settle the argument.
The report is an affirmation of our earlier findings.

senses.definition: A positive statement that you repeat to encourage yourself or change your way of thinking.
examples: She starts each day with a short affirmation.
Positive affirmations can help build confidence.

senses.definition: In law, a formal promise that something is true, made instead of an oath.
examples: Witnesses may make an affirmation rather than swear an oath.
She gave an affirmation that the contents were correct.

senses.definition: Plural of affirmation: positive statements that people repeat to encourage themselves.
examples: She writes her affirmations on sticky notes around the house.
Daily affirmations can support mental well‑being.

senses.definition: Plural of affirmation: strong statements that something is true.
examples: Their affirmations of support were encouraging.
Official affirmations from the committee ended the doubts.

senses.definition: Showing agreement or saying yes.
examples: We received an affirmative response from the committee.
The vote was largely affirmative.

senses.definition: Supporting a policy that helps groups who have been treated unfairly in the past.
examples: The university has an affirmative action policy for admissions.
Affirmative measures aim to increase diversity in the workplace.

senses.definition: A word or statement that means yes.
examples: He replied in the affirmative.
An affirmative from the board will allow the project to start.

senses.definition: In a way that shows agreement or says yes.
examples: She nodded affirmatively when asked if she understood.
The committee voted affirmatively on the proposal.

senses.definition: The quality of being positive or definite in stating something.
examples: His affirmativeness made others trust his decisions.
Too much affirmativeness can sometimes seem rigid.

senses.definition: Words or statements that mean yes or show agreement.
examples: The chair counted ten affirmatives and two negatives.
Short affirmatives like "sure" or "okay" can signal agreement.

senses.definition: Showing or giving support to the truth of something.
examples: The witness gave affirmatory evidence.
An affirmatory statement was included in the contract.

senses.definition: Past tense of affirm: said firmly that something is true.
examples: She affirmed that the data were accurate.
They affirmed their commitment to the project.

senses.definition: Past tense of affirm in law: said that a judgment or decision is correct.
examples: The appeals court affirmed the lower court's ruling.
The conviction was affirmed by the highest court.

senses.definition: A person who states firmly that something is true or expresses support.
examples: She is a strong affirmer of human rights.
Each affirmer signed the statement of principles.

senses.definition: People who state firmly that something is true or express support.
examples: The affirmers of the policy spoke first at the meeting.
Many affirmers of the movement gathered in the city square.

senses.definition: Present participle of affirm: saying firmly that something is true.
examples: The witness was affirming that he saw the suspect at the scene.
The authors are affirming the importance of early education.

senses.definition: Giving support, confidence, or a sense of value.
examples: Her affirming words helped him feel less anxious.
A warm, affirming environment is important for children.

senses.definition: In a way that shows firm agreement or support.
examples: She smiled affirmingly as he spoke.
The audience nodded affirmingly during the speech.

senses.definition: Non‑standard or rare: in a firm and positive way.
examples: He spoke affirmly, leaving no room for doubt.
She affirmly rejected the accusations.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of affirm: says firmly that something is true.
examples: The report affirms that climate change is already affecting the region.
He affirms his innocence despite the accusations.

senses.definition: To fasten or stick one thing onto another.
examples: Please affix a recent photograph to the application form.
The notice was affixed to the front door.

senses.definition: To add a letter or group of letters to the beginning or end of a word.
examples: You can affix the prefix "un‑" to make the opposite meaning.
In this exercise, students affix different suffixes to the base word.

senses.definition: A letter or group of letters added to the beginning or end of a word to change its meaning.
examples: The affix "re‑" often means to do something again.
In the word "happiness", "‑ness" is an affix that turns an adjective into a noun.

senses.definition: That can be fastened, stuck, or attached to something.
examples: The label is affixable to most smooth surfaces.
Only affixable items should be placed in this section.

senses.definition: Relating to an affix or to the process of adding affixes to words.
examples: Affixal morphology is an important part of this language's grammar.
The student confused root changes with affixal changes.

senses.definition: The process of adding letters or groups of letters to a word to change its meaning or form.
examples: Affixation is one of the most common ways to create new words in English.
The book gives many examples of affixation with prefixes and suffixes.

senses.definition: The act of fastening or attaching something, especially in a formal or technical context.
examples: Proper affixation of hazard labels is required by law.
The instructions describe the affixation of the seal to the container.
